CHAPTER 1 .
AN ACT to require agricultural cooperative association on re
quest to furnish information as to membership and contracts
and providing for the effector refusal or compliance with such
requests or failure to make such requests .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§1 . Upon applicationin writingby registered
mail to any agriculturalco-operative marketing as-
sociationdoing business under the laws of thisCom-
monwealth by any landowner, lessor or lesseeof
land on which any agriculturalcrops marketed by
such associationare being raised or are to be raised,
asking for information as to whether a certainper-
son or persons are members of such association, or
have signed itsmarketing agreement or asking for
informationas to the terms of said contract, said
associationshall within five days after receipt of
said request furnish said information in writing to
the person or persons inquiringat the address given
in the application.
§2 . Such applicationshallgive the fullname of
the person or persons concerning whom the infor

2 COMMONWEALTH OF KENTUCKY
mation is requestedand theirpost officeaddresses,
and shallcontaina returnaddressof the person or
persons to whom the information is to be sent.
§3 . Unless such information as above requested
shallbe furnishedby saidassociation,in any litiga-
tion which may arise between such associationand
the person or persons inquiring, it shall be pre-
sumed , in the absence of evidence to the contrary,
that the person requesting said information was
without noticethat the person inquired about was a
member of such associationor had signed such mar-
ketingcontract, or that the person inquiringknew
the terms of the contract .
§4 . Unless such landlord, lessor or lessee shall
have made the above inquiry and waited until the
expiration of the time above set forth or until the
receiptof answer thereto, in any litigationarising
with such co-operativeassociation, itshallbe con-
clusivelypresumed that such person has noticeof
thecontractor membership obligationsactuallyex-
isting between the associationand its member or
obligor.
§5 . Whereas , there are many thousand co-oper-
ative contracts now in force in this state and lack
of information createsconfusion as to the rights of
members and non -members thereunder, an emer-
gency is declaredto existand thisact shallbe in
forcefrom and afteritspassage and approvalby the
Governor .
Neither approved nor disapproved .
CHAPTER 2 .
AN ACT appropriating twenty -five thousand dollars annually
for the establishment , operation and maintenance of a sub-
experiment station under the direction of the Agricultural
Experiment Station of the University of Kentucky , prescrib

ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY 3
ing the purpose of the sub -experiment station and the man-
ner in which the appropriation is to be paid .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§1 . There is hereby establishedon and in con-
nection with the lands donated to the University of
Kentucky by the E. O. Robinson Mountain Fund ,
a sub-experiment stationto be known as the Robin-
son Sub -experiment Station. Said sub-experiment
station is to be a division of and to be directed and
operatedby theAgriculturalExperiment Stationof
the Universityof Kentucky,heretoforeestablished
by an act of the general assembly .
§2 . It shall be the object and duty of the said
sub-experiment station to conduct experimental
work and carry on investigationsin timberproduc-
tion, forestgrowth, forestrenewal and the utiliza-
tion of forestproducts, and itshallmake such ex-
periments as may determine the practicabilityof
reforestationand the development of a permanent
forestindustry. The said sub-experiment station
shallalsoconductexperimentalwork,investigations
and demonstrations in the various phases of horti-
culture,animalhusbandry and agricultureand such
other investigationsand demonstrations as bear
directly upon the development of the agricultural
industry of eastern Kentucky .
$3. For the purpose of providingthe necessary
funds for thepayment of salaries,costof mainten-
ance and operation of the said sub-experiment sta-
tionand forcarryingout theprovisionsof thisact,
thereishereby appropriatedto theKentucky Agri-
culturalExperiment Station out of the funds of the
statenot otherwiseappropriated,thesum oftwenty-
fivethousand dollars($25,000.00) annually. Such
salariesand expenses of operationand mainten

4 COMMONWEALTH OF KENTUCKY
ance as may be necessary for the carrying out of the
provisionsof thisact shallbe paid from said sum
hereinappropriated,by warrant on the auditorof
public accounts drawn by the director of the Ken-
tucky AgriculturalExperiment Station and counter-
signed by the business agent of the University of
Kentucky ,and saidbusinessagentshallkeep proper
vouchers on filein his officefor allexpenditures.
Approved March 8, 1924 .
CHAPTER 3 .
AN ACT to provide for the establishment and maintenance of a

Commonwealth of Kentucky :

culturalexperiment stationof the Universityof
Kentucky, to be known as the Western Kentucky
sub-experimentstation, be and the same ishereby
establishednear Princeton, in Caldwellcounty, on
a tract of four hundred (400) acres of land to be
donated by the citizensof said county, for the pur-
pose of advancing,fosteringand promoting agricul-
turalinterest, and conductingexperimentwork,in-
vestigationand demonstrations in the various
phases of agriculture, horticulture, animal hus-
bandry and such other investigationsand demon-
strationsas bear directlyupon the development of
the agriculturalinterestsof Kentucky, and to be
under the controland supervision of the directorof
theKentucky AgriculturalExperiment Station.

funds for the payment of salaries,costof mainten-
ance and operation of the said sub-experiment sta-

thereishereby appropriatedto the Kentucky Agri-
culturalExperiment Station,out of thefunds of the

ies and expenses of operation and maintenance as

CHAPTER 7 .
AN ACT entitled an act permitting the members of co -operative
marketing associations to execute crop mortgages upon their
products ; and defining the rights of mortgagors and mort-
gagees of crops covered by said marketing agreements ; and
providing a method for the sale of said crops and the applica-
tion of the proceeds thereof under attachment and foreclosure
proceedings ; and declaring an emergency to exist.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§1 . That growers of agriculturalproducts who
have signed contracts with co-operative marketing
associations, authorizedby the Bingham Co-opera-
tiveMarketingAct,be,and they are hereby permit-
ted toplace crop mortgages upon crops in esse; but
said crop mortgages shallnot defeat the rightof
any associationorganized under the co-operative
act to take deliveryof any crop covered by a mar-
ketingcontract. Any grower executingsuch a crop
mortgage may make an assignmenttothemortgagee

10 COMMONWEALTH OF KENTUCKY
of the grower's interestin the proceeds of such crop,
subjectto the provisionof said marketing agree-
ment ,which assignment must be accepted by any as-
sociationorganized under said act.
§2 . In the event of foreclosure, attachment or
proceedingin courtconcerningany crop coveredby
such marketing agreement and mortgage,itshallbe
the duty of the court to directthat the crop shall
be deliveredto and soldby the saidmarketing asso-
ciationaccording to the terms of the contract and
the proceeds derived therefrom so far as may be
necessaryto satisfythe judgment in the case shall
be paid as directedby the judgment of the court.
§3 . All laws or parts of laws in conflictherewith
are hereby repealed.
$§4 . Whereas ,thereisa greatconfusionas to the
rightsand duties of mortgagors and mortgagees of
agriculturalproductsin thisCommonwealth , when
such mortgagors have become members of co-opera-
tiveassociations,and whereas such confusion iscon-
ducivetoneedlesslitigation,an emergency ishereby
declaredto exist,and thisactshallbecome effective
immediatelyupon itspassage and signatureby the
Governor .
Approved March 29 ,1924 .

CHAPTER 9 .
AN ACT creating a Kentucky State Park Commission .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§1 . There ishereby createdthe Kentucky State

14 COMMONWEALTH OF KENTUCKY
Park Commission of threemembers ,of which Com-
mission the Director of the Kentucky State Geologi-
cal Survey shallbe one and shallbe chairman , the
second member thereof to be the Dean of the State
AgriculturalCollege, and the thirda commissioner
to be appointedby the Governor, with the consent
of the Senate .
§2 . It shallbe the duty of the Commission hereby
created to ascertainbetween now and the meeting
of the General Assembly in 1926 any suitableand
availableState park sites, with a view to securing
them by public subscription and later such park
sites, with the approval of the legislature, to be
turnedovertotheStatefortheirpreservation,beau-
tificationand maintenance . Should the Commission
hereby created find it impracticable to complete its
labor before the session of the General Assembly
which shallconvenein 1926,itshallneverthelessre-
port itsacts to that sessionand an additionalterm
of two years,ifnecessary,is given to completeits
labors .
§3 . Said Commission shall serve without com-
pensation. The members thereof shall be allowed
theirexpensesin making any necessarypark inves-
tigationsand for officialprinting. Said expenses
are to be certifiedby the chairman of the commission
to the Auditor of Public Accounts of the State of
Kentucky ,and the commission shallreportitsfind-
ings to the Governor and the General Assembly in
1926,subjectto such extensionof time as issetout
in Section 2 hereof .
Approved March 28, 1924 .
CHAPTER 10 .
AN ACT regulating the sales of leaf tobacco at public auction
in this Commonwealth . 7

ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY 15
Be it enactedby the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
That Section4814b-1 to 4 inclusive, Kentucky
Statutes, Carroll'sEdition1915,be and the same is
hereby repealed and the following is enacted in lieu
thereof :
§1 . That itshallbe the duty of any tobacco ware-
houseman ,corporation,firmor individual,who shall
receive,or who shallundertake to receiveor take care
of leaftobacco,for saleat publicauction, whether
with or withoutcompensationor reward,to post or
cause to be posted,a notice,in a conspicuousplace
upon the premises of saidwarehouseman , corpora-
tion, firm or individual, statingthe number of
pounds intheaggregate,actuallysold,and the aver-
age price per pound received on account of each
day's sale. It shallfurther be the duty of said
warehousemen , prior to any such sale, to post at a
pointin the officeof saidwarehouse,convenientand
accessiblefor publicinspection, a typewrittenor
printedlistshowing the true name and postoffice
address of the owner and producer and the number
of pounds oftobaccoof eachperson,firmor corpor-
ation,whose tobacco willthat day be offeredfor sale
in said warehouse . No tobacco shall be delivered to
or receivedby any warehousemen for saleat public
auction, unless the true names and postofficead-
dresses of the producer and owner of such tobacco
are furnished said warehousemen by the person de-
liveringsame .
§2 . It shallbe the duty of said warehousemen to
post said notice of sale not later than nine o'clock
a.m .on the day followingsuch saleor sales;to post
saidlistof tobaccoto be thatday sold,not lessthan
thirtyminutes prior to saleof tobacco of any owner
or producer .

16 COMMONWEALTH OF KENTUCKY
§3 . Any warehousemen ,corporation,firmor in-
dividualwho shallfailor refuseto post the said
noticesin accordance with the provisionsof thisact
shall be subject to a fine in a sum not less than
$50.00, nor more than $100.00, for each day for so
failing or refusing .
§4 . Any warehousemen , corporation, firm or in-
dividualwho shallin saidnoticefalsifythe actual
number of pounds sold or the average price thereof,
or shallfalselylistthe name , postofficeaddress or
number of pounds of tobacco of any producer or
owner whose tobacco will that day be offered for
sale, or shallfurnish a falsename or address of the
owner or producer to any warehousemen , shallbe
subjectto indictmentand upon convictionshallbe
fined $500.00 for each offense.
§5 . Owing to the existenceof unnecessary litiga-
tion and confusion growing out of the delivery and
sale of tobaccoin Kentucky ,an emergency ishereby
declared to exist and this act shall take effect from
and afteritspassage and approval by the Governor .
Approved February 27 , 1924 .

and trust companies organized under the laws of Kentucky ,

pany , or any agent or agents of either or any such ; and mak-

CHAPTER 24 .
AN ACT relating to the office of Commonwealth's Attorney in
Counties having a population of Two Hundred Thousand , or
over , and providing for Assistants , and fixing their salaries.
Be itenacted by the General Assembly of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky :
§1 . That the offices of First Assistant Com-
monwealth's Attorney and Second Assistant Com-
monwealth'sAttorney in countieshaving a popula-
tion of two hundred thousand , or over, are hereby
created .
§2 . Said First Assistant Commonwealth's At-
torney and said Second Assistant Commonwealth's
Attorney shallbe appointedby theCommonwealth's
Attorney for a term of four years,but they shallbe
subjectto removal at any time by the Common-
wealth's Attorney . The salary of the First Assist-
ant Commonwealth's Attorney shallbe three thous-
and fivehundred dollarsper annum and the salary
of the Second Assistant Commonwealth's Attorney
shallbe three thousand dollarsper annum , payable
out of the county levy in equal monthly installments.
§3 . Each of the Assistant Commonwealth's At-
torneysprovidedforby thisactshallbe a citizenof
Kentucky and shall,at the time of appointment,re-
side in the district in which the Commonwealth's
Attorney iselectedand shallbe a licensedpractic-
ing lawyer. Said AssistantCommonwealth's Attor-
ney shallhave the same powers and perform the
same dutiesthat Commonwealth's Attorneys now
have and perform , subjectto the directionsand con-
trolof the Commonwealth's Attorney. They shall
aid the Commonwealth's Attorney in all matters
pertainingtohisoffice,and insuchmanner ashe may
from time to time designate.
§4 . In the absence of the Commonwealth's At

ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY 39
torney, the FirstAssistantCommonwealth's Attor-
ney shallact as Commonwealth's Attorney , without
additionalcompensation ; and in the absence of both
the Commonwealth's Attorney and theFirstAssist-
ant Commonwealth's Attorney, the Second Assist-
ant Commonwealth's Attorney shallact as Common-
wealth'sAttorney, without aditionalcompensation.
§5 . The officeof Assistant Commonwealth's At-
torneycreatedby an actofMarch eighteen,nineteen
hundred and two, which act is now sectionone
hundred and twenty-fiveA of the Kentucky Statutes,
editedby Honorable John D. Carroll, sixth edition,
isabolished,and saidactofMarch eighteen,nineteen
hundred and two ,being said sectionone hundred and
twenty-fiveA , ishereby repealed, and allactsand
parts of actsin conflictherewith are hereby repealed.
In order to relievethe congestion of the docket in
circuitcourtsincountieshaving a populationof two
hundred thousand or over, and in order to provide
for the immediate reliefof such congested condition
of the docket, an emergency is hereby declared to
exist and this act shall become effective from and
afteritspassage and approval by the Governor .
Approved March 28, 1924.

CHAPTER 33 .
AN ACT to provide for the appointment of special judges ex-
traordinary to preside over the circuit courts in this Common-
wealth , fix their compensation and define the conditions under
which they may be appointed and the jurisdiction which they
may exercise. This Act is to be known as section Nine hun-
dred and seventy -one -seven of Kentucky Statutes .
Be it enactedby the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§1 . That whenever it shall appear to the Gov-
ernor of thisCommonwealth thatthe regularjudge
of the circuitcourtin any county isunable to hold
any regular or special term of his court in said
county or try any case pending in said court, and
the Governor in the exercise of a sound discretion is
not able to secure a regular circuitjudge in this
Commonwealth to act as specaljudge to hold said
term of court or to try said case,as now provided by
law ,thatthe Governor of thisCommonwealth be then
authorized and empowered to appoint a special
judge extraordinary and grant to him a commission
to go into such county to hold the term of court or
to try the case .
§2 . The specialjudge extraordinaryso ap-
pointedby the Governor shallbe an attorneywith
the constitutionalrequirementsof a circuitjudge.
§3 . The specialjudge extraordinary, when so
appointedand commissionedby the Governor,shall
have allof the power and authorityof the regular
circuitjudge to go intothe county and to hold the

54 COMMONWEALTH OF KENTUCKY
regularterm or thespecialterm of thecircuitcourt,
or to try thecase in saidcounty, for which he has
been commissioned,and when a specialjudge extra-
ordinary has been commissioned to hold a regular
term of thecircuitcourtin any countyof thisCom-
monwealth,such regularterm may be continuedby
the specialjudge extraordinaryfrom day to day
afterthe date fixedby law for the closeof such reg-
ular term untilthe business of such regular term of
the circuitcourt in such county iscompleted without
further orders for the trialof any cases upon the
docket of said court .
§4 . The specialjudge extraordinaryshallbe
paid as compensation for his servicesthe sum of
fourteendollars($14.00) per day for the timehe is
engaged in holding such term of court or in trying
such cases, be allowed and paid to him as provided
by law for the payment of claims against thisCom-
monwealth upon an order of his court showing the
number of days 'serviceso rendered by him in hold-
ing such court or in the trialof such case.
§5 . In view of thenecessityfor speedilyprovid-
ing means for the trialof causes in which regular
judge of such courtis unable to sit, an emergency
is declaredto exist, and thisact shalltake effect
from and afteritsapproval by the Governor .
Neither approved nor disapproved .

CHAPTER 54 .
AN ACT to require the teaching of the Constitution of the United
States in the public and private schools of the State of Ken-
tucky .
Be itenacted by the General Assembly of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky :
§1 . In all public and private schools located
within the Stateof Kentucky,commencing with the
school year next ensuing after the passage of this
act, there shallbe given regular courses of instruc-
tion in the Constitution of the United States .

88 COMMONWEALTH OF KENTUCKY
§2 . Such instruction in the Constitution of the
United States shallbegin not laterthan the opening
of eighth grade, and shall continue in the high
school course, and in courses in State colleges, uni-
versitiesand the educational departments of State
and municipal institutionsto an extent to be deter-
mined by the Superintendentof PublicInstruction.
§3 . This act shall become effective from and
afteritspassage and approval,as providedby law,
and alllaws and parts of laws in conflictherewith
are hereby repealed.
Approved March 22, 1924 .

CHAPTER 59 .
AN ACT providing for the reading of the Bible in the Common
Schools of the State of Kentucky .
Whereas , the rules and regulations governing the reading of
the Holy Bible in the common schools of this Commonwealth
are not uniform , and whereas religion and morality are necessary
to good government and the Bible is civilization'stext book of
morality and it is important the common school children shall
have lessons of morality brought to their attention during their
school days , therefore ,
Be itenacted by the General Assembly of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky :
§1 . The teacher in charge shallread or cause to
be read a portionof the Bible,daily,in every class
room or session room of the common schools of the
Stateof Kentucky,in thepresenceofpupilstherein
assembled,and no childshallbe requiredtoread the
Bibleagainstthewish ofhisparentor guardian.
§2 . The failureof any teacherto conform to

ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY 109
this Act shall be cause for the revocation of his cer-
tificatein the manner provided by law .
Approved March 22, 1924 .

CHAPTER 62 .
AN ACT authorizing the boards of education of the different
cities in Kentucky and the county boards of education and
the boards of trustees of independent graded school districts
to provide for the education of children with defective eye-
sight, and for such purpose to provide special instruction for
such children , and to secure and provide suitable books for
such children .
Be itenactedby theGeneralAssembly oftheCom-
monwealth of Kentucky :
§1 . The board of education of any city in this
Commonwealth and the board of education of any
county and the board of trusteesof any independent
graded schooldistrictare hereby authorized to pro-
vide for the instruction and education of children of
proper school age who , by reason of defectiveeye-
sightrequirespecialbooks or specialinstructionor
both inorderprofitablyand safelytoattendthepub-
licschoolsin such cityor countyor district.
§2 . The boards of education of such cities,
countiesand districts,for thepurpose of givingin-
structionto childrenhaving such defectiveeyesight,
are authorizedto provide for and directthe teachers
in the severalschoolsthereof, as said boards may
deem proper, to establishand maintain classesfor
the instructionand education of such pupils having
defectiveeyesight; and if such classesare so estab-
lished, the said boards of education of said cities,
countiesand districtsshallbe authorizedto purchase
and acquire such special equipment and books as
may be proper and necessary for the use and instruc-
tionof suchpupilson accountof theirdefectiveeye-
sight; and such specialbooks may be purchased and
provided by said boards,or may be recommended to
the parentsof such childrenfor purchase, and for
the use of such children, although said books may
not be included in the list of books selected for other

ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY 125
childrenby the Text Book Commission, or by the
board of educationor by the board of trustees.
§3 . Pupils with such defectiveeyesight, as
above mentioned , for whom special books may be
provided or recommended , shallnot be requiredto
purchase or use the books adopted for general use
for the other pupils unless such books as have been
adopted for generaluse may be used by the pupils
with defectiveeyesight,withoutinjurythereto; and
the instructionof such pupils with defectiveeyesight
may be so arranged that instructioncan be given to
them separately,or in connectionwith otherclasses,
as may be deemed proper and best.
§4 . The board of educationof any cityor of any
countyor of any district,as hereinbeforementioned,
shallnot undertake to provide for the instructionof
pupilswithdefectiveeyesightand to purchasebooks
therefor, except by permission and in pursuance of
the advice and authorityof the State Superintendent
of Public Instruction; and such Superintendentof
PublicInstructionshallgivesuchadviceand permis-
sion as he may deem proper for such purposes , and
shallprepare and distributeinformation to the dif-
ferent boards of education of said cities, counties
and districtsconcerning the improved methods of
such instruction, and the qualificationsof teachers
for such classes; and such Superintendent may re-
quire annual reportsfrom such boards as shallhave
caused classesand instructionto be provided for
such pupils;and said Superintendent may designate
one or more of his assistantsto supervise the classes
and instructionof childrenwith defectiveeyesight,
as herein provided for.
§5 . Any board of educationof any city,county
or district, as hereinprovidedfor,may collectand
expend such funds as may be proper and suitable
for such instructionfor childrenof defectiveeye

126 COMMONWEALTH OF KENTUCKY
sight,and for thepurchase of equipment and books,
as provided for in this act, in the same manner as
funds are raised and expended for the maintenance
of other school activities.
§6 . Nothing in thisact shallbe so construed as
to annul any law or regulationthatmay preclude
from attendance upon the schools children afflicted
with communicable eye diseases.
§7 . This act shall take effectfrom and after its
passageand approval,as providedby law.
Approved March 26 , 1924 .

CHAPTER 64 .
AN ACT relating to registration for elections in citiesand towns
having a population of 5,000 inhabitants or more .

ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY 137
Be itenacted by the General Assembly of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky :
§1 . Every citizenof the United States,not other-
wise disqualifiedby thelaws of thisCommonwealth ,
who istwenty-one years of age,or who willbecome
twenty -one years of age on or before the next regu-
lar election to be held in Novermer or on or before
the date of any specialelection,who willhave been a
residentof the State of Kentucky one year, and of
the county sixmonths,and of the precinctinwhich
he or she offersto vote sixty days next preceding
such electionand who has been registered as pro-
vided by thisact, and who is otherwisequalified,
shallbe entitledto vote for allofficerselectiveby the
people and on allmeasures or questions submitted
for determination by the voters at any regular or
specialelection.
§2 . It shallbe the duty of the Board of Election
Commissioners of each countyin which islocateda
cityor town of the first,second,third or fourth class,
not laterthan the20thday ofAugust,1924,and an-
nually thereafter, to appoint for each registration
precinct in said city or town two judges, one clerk,
and one sheriffof registrationto act as such in their
precincts,allof whom shallhold theirofficesfor one
year, or untiltheirsuccessorsare appointed and
qualified. The County Executive Committee of the
two politicalpartieshaving representationon the
State Board and County Boards of Election Com-

CHAPTER 67 .
AN ACT appropriating money to pay certain expenses authorized
by the State Registration Board , created by act of the General
Assembly of 1922 , and which act was later declared invalid
by the courts, but only after said expenses had been incurred
in good faith and the work directed had been performed .
Whereas , the General Assembly of Kentucky , session of 1922 ,
enacted a general registration law and created a State Registra-
tion Board with certain specific duties to perform , among them
the preparation and distribution of certain registration books
and forms containing printed matter to all of the election pre-
cincts of the Commonwealth , said act being known as House
Bill 193 , introduced January 23, 1922 , page 280, volume one ,
House Journal , 1922 session , and which act is Chapter 138 of the
Session Acts of 1922 , and
Whereas , the State Registration Board created under this law
met and organized following said session and let a contract for
producing and distributing the registration books and printed
forms , to the several counties of the Commonwealth , fixing the

ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY 159
prices to be paid therefor, said contract being let to the Standard
Printing Company , of Louisville, Ky ., and
Whereas , the said Standard Printing Company , with the ap-
proval of the said State Registration Board , and because of the
fact that no single printing and publishing house in the Com-
monwealth could execute the order within the brief time al-
lowed under the law for printing and distribution, sublet one-
third of the total amount of its contract each to the Bradley-
Gilbert Company and to the C. T. Dearing Printing Company ,
both of Louisville, Ky ., and
Whereas , after the completion and distribution of this work in
good faith, and carrying out the orders of said State Registra-
tion Board for same , but before same had been paid for, a suit
was instituted raisng the question of the constitutionality of the
legislative act providing for general registration, under which
said State Registration Board had acted in directing the per-
formance of this work , and , in determining this suit the act in
question was declared unconstitutional and void , and this just
debt has not yet been satisfied by the Commonwealth , now ,
therefore ,
Be itenacted by the General Assembly of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky :
That upon thepresentationof approved billsfrom
the said Standard Printing Company for $30,312.74;
theBradley-GilbertCompany for$29,758.23;and the
C. T. Dearing PrintingCompany for$29,905.38,said
billshaving the approval of the State Superintend-
ent of Public Printing as being in accordance with
the contractmade by the said State Registration
Board ,the Auditorof PublicAccounts be,and he is
hereby authorized to draw his warrants on the
Treasurer to pay same in the manner provided by
law for the payment of claims against the Common-
wealth .
Approved March 28 , 1924 .

tics,' approved March 17, 1902 , and being section 33-a of the

fications, duties and penalties for interference and providing

utes, Carroll'sEditionof 1922 be repealed; and in

organizedas follows,and thefollowingenacted:
§2 . Personnel of the Department. In the Bu-
reau of Agriculture, Labor and Statisticsthere shall

proval of the Governor as specifiedin Section4, a
chieflaber inspector,who may be eithera man or a
woman , and four deputy labor inspectors, two of
whom shallbe men and two shallbe women , who
with one clericalassistant, shall constitutethe De

partment of Labor under the Commissioner. The
Chief Labor Inspector shall receive $3,000.00per

monthly. The salaryof the clericalassistantshall
not exceed $1,200.00per annum .
§3 . Qualificationsof the Chief Labor Inspector.
The Chief Labor Inspector shallbe a person with
a good working knowledge of shop and factory
practice, industrialsanitation, accident prevention,
the general industrialdevelopment in the State,and
of the labor laws of the state, and shall also be a

in complete charge of the activitiesof the Depart-
ment of Labor , and together with his deputies shall

§4 . CompetitiveExamination of Applicantsfor
Chiefand Deputy Inspectorships. Every personde-
siringappointment eitheras chiefor deputy labor
inspector shall be required to pass a competitive
written and oral examination touching his or her
qualificationsas set forth in section3. The records
of such examinations(includingquestions, answers
and marks awarded) shallbe keptintheDepartment
as a matter of publicrecord and open to the inspec-
tionof any citizenat any and alltimes. Such exam-
inationshallbe held by the Commissioner ,the State
Sanitary Engineer and the Head of the Engineer-
ing Department of the Universityof Kentucky . The
Chief Inspector shall be chosen from among four
applicants receiving the highest rating. Any va-
cancy occurringin the officeof chiefor deputy in-
spectors shallbe filledin the same manner as ap-
pointments are made in the firstinstance.
Acts - 6

162 COMMONWEALTH OF KENTUCKY
§5 . Causes and Procedure for Removal from
Office. The Chief Inspector and the Deputy Inspec-
tors shall be appointed for a period of four years
and may be removed from officeby the Commis-
sionerfor neglectof duty, incompetence, gross in-
subordination, intoxication, or immorality: Pro-
vided,thatsuch removal shallnot take place untilat
leastfivedays after a written notice specifyingthe
charges against him or her shallbe sent by regis-
tered mail to the offending inspector who may de-
mand a publichearing of saidcharges. A copy of

and shallbe a matter of public record and a record
shallaso be kept of the proceedings of the hearings
and any party feelingaggrieved may appeal to the
Governor ,who may reverse the findingsof the Com-
missioner on the records .
§6 . Duties of the Department . The Department
of Labor shallthroughitsinspectorsvisitplacesof
employment for the purposes of (1) the investiga-
tion of the conditionof employment affectingthe
life, health, safety, of employes, (2) the adminis-
tration and enforcement of alllaws of the state regu-
latingthe employment of labor,and (3) the collec-
tionof statisticspertainingthereto. Any employer
shallbe furnished,upon requestmade to the Chief
Labor Inspector,a detailedreportof any inspection
of that employer's place of business.
§7 . Records. Proper, complete and permanent
recordsshallbe made of allinvestigationsshowing
dates when examinationswere made , the condition
in which the establishment was found and what
changes were ordered. Such records shall be de-
liveredby the ChiefLabor Inspector,at the expira-

sionerwho shallgive to the Chief Labor Inspector
a properlyattestedreceipttherefor.

§8 . Interference with Inspectors. Any em-
ployer, firm or corporation, or officer, manager or

who shall refuse or attempt to prevent the admis-
sionof any inspectorauthorizedby thisarticle,upon
or within the premises or building of any establish-

bedy a complete summary of the work of the depart-
ment ,includingnumber of visitsmade ,typesof es-
tablishmentsinspected, working conditionsfound,
number ofviolationsoflaborlaws,number ofprose-
cutions,and improvements noted.

ecutingattorneyof each county and cityin thisState

`ing the salariesof allpersons appointedunder it,

forthedepartment,upon the submissionof an item-
izedstatementof same duly certifiedby the Chief

Labor Inspector. The biennialreportsof the De-
partment of Labor, also an adequate number of

§15 . Mine Inspectors Not Affected. Nothing in

$ 16. Workmen's Compensation Board Not Af-
fected. Nothing in this act shallbe construed to

atelyafteritspassage and approval by the Gov-
ernor .

CHAPTER 71 .
AN ACT to regulate the issue and redemption of script or other
evidence of debt in order to prohibit the graft from workmen's
wages for advancement before pay day .
Be it enactedby the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§1 . That an individual,firm,partnership,or or-
ganizationor corporation,employinglaborwho may
hereafterissueany script,due bills,checks,or other
evidenceof debt, in any form, for labor shallre-
deem same in cash or legal tender at face value at
leastonce in each month on a regular pay day from
any person or persons,firm or corporationwho may
presentthe same for payment. Provided,that any
person, firm or corporation, buying said scriptor

180 COMMONWEALTH OF KENTUCKY
other evidence of debt which has been issued to em-
ployees for labor shallbe entitledto sue the person ,
firm or corporation issuing the same if payment is
refused, and shallbe entitledto recover face value
thereforifithas been paid for in goods and mer-
chandise in store, and if paid for in cash, shallbe
entitledto recover the amount paid for said script
or other evidence of debt issued to employes , to-
gether with six per cent interestfrom date said
scriptwas purchased , and in the event said amount
paid for said scriptor other evidence of debt issued
toemployees,islessthanthefacevaluethereof,and
that the amount paid and interestthereon is less
tan face value of said script,the residueof the face
value thereof shall be credited on the books of the
employer to the employee to whom it was issued
and said employee shallbe entitledto receive same
on any regular pay day of said employer ; and pro-
vided, that the person, firm or corporation, suing
said employer to recover on said scriptor other evi-
dence of debt shallnot be required to make the per-
sons from whom said scriptor other evidence of debt
was purchased , party or parties to any action
broughttoenforcecollectionfor same . Allpersons,
firmsand corporations, purchasingscriptor other
evidence of debt issued to employes on account of
labor shallkeep an accurate record of the amount
of script or other evidence of debt purchased
and thisrecord so kept shallshow the name of each
person from whom scriptor other evidence of debt
issuedto employees for labor was purchased, the
amount purchased,datethereof,and amount paid in
goods or in cash or other thing of value,and who is-
sued said script or other evidence of debt so pur-
chased. This act shallnot apply to persons,firms or
corporationsemploying lessthan twenty persons.
The itemizedstatement from the record so kept shall

ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY 181
be presentedwhen payment isdemanded for script
or other evidenceof debt, so purchased from any
person ,firm or corporation and said statement shall
be properly sworn to by the person presenting same ,
or by his or itsagent.
Approved March 12 , 1924 .
CHAPTER 72 .
AN ACT to repeal section 2711a -128 , Kentucky Statutes ; to
create and make appropriation for the Armory Fund ; to create
the Armory Commission of Kentucky and define its powers ;
to require said Commission to provide adequate armories and
facilitiesfor the Kentucky National Guard and authorizing
said Commission to expend the armory fund for such purposes ;
and to authorize counties , cities and towns to acquire and
maintain buildings suitable for public assemblage and other
public purposes , including armory purposes , and to levy taxes
for their acquisition and maintenance , and to maintain such
buildings actually used for armory purposes ; to grant the
power of condemnation to the Armory Commission and to
counties , citiesand towns , and prescribe the procedure there-
for; to provide assembly rooms for veterans ' associations; to
repeal all laws in conflict with this act; and to declare an
emergency .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
That Section2711a-128,Kentucky Statutes,being
Section128 of Chapter 43,Acts of 1916, approved
March 23,1916,be and the same ishereby repealed
and that the following be enacted in lieu thereof :
§1 . There ishereby created a body corporate to
be known as the Armory Commission of Kentucky ,
the members of which shall be the Governor , who
shallbe chairman, the Adjutant General, and the
threehighest ranking officerson the activelistof the
Kentucky National Guard . Said Commission may
in itscorporate name sue and be sued, contract and

182 COMMONWEALTH OF KENTUCKY
be contractedwith,acquirerealand personalprop-
erty by gift,purchase or condemnation ; and may do
allthings and exerciseallrightsand powers reason-
ably necessary to carry out and perform the duties
intendedor requiredby thisact.
The members shall serve without compensation ,
and shall be allowed their reasonable expenses in-
curred in attending meetings of the Commission or
while travelingunder orders for the performance of
duty in connectionwith the business of said Commis-
sion .
Said Commission shallhold regular meetings at
Frankfort on thefirstMonday inMarch ,June,Sep-
tember and December ofeachyear,and specialmeet-
ings on callof the Governor , after reasonable no-
tice. A majority of the members shall constitute
a quorum for the considerationof business,but all
contracts and agreements creating liabilitiesor in
debtedness against,or requiringappropriation from
theArmory Fund hereinafterin Section4 provided,
shallrequiretheaffirmativevoteof a majorityof all
members of the Commission . In the absence of the
Governor or Adjutant General,the Lieutenant Gov-
ernor or AssistantAdjutant General may act for
them , respectively.
§2 . The Armory Commission shallprovide ade-
quate armories,buildingsand grounds for theKen-
tucky NationalGuard for itspurposes of drill, in-
structionand administration and for the safekeep-
ing of publicproperty of the State and the United
States issued to or for the use of the Kentucky Na-
tionalGuard , including animals, and for such pur-
pose shallbe charged with the administration and
expenditureof theArmory Fund hereinafterin Sec-
tion 4 provided . All expenditures from said fund
authorizedby the Commission shallbe made upon

ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY 183
vouchers authenticatedby the Adjutant General or
Assistant Adjutant General.
§3 . The Armory Commission may ,eitheralone
or in co-operationwith counties,citiesor incorpor-
atedtowns,privatecorporations,voluntaryunincor-
porated associationsor individuals, construct, or
acquireby purchase, contract,lease, gift,donation
or condemnation, armories, buildingsor grounds
suitablefor drill,instructionand administrationand
the safekeepingof publicproperty,and make addi-
tions and improvements in or to such armories and
facilities,and eitheralone or with the likeco-opera-
tion of others provide heat, light, water, telephone
service, and other costs of operation and mainten-
ance, includinginsurance; provided, that the cost
of constructionor of purchase of armories and of
grounds for the use of one unit shall not exceed
$10,000, and $5,000 for each additionalunit at the
same stationand using the same facilities, insofar
as such cost shall be charged against the Armory
Fund ,provided further that in the discretionof the
Armory Commission, these amounts may be in-
creased to $15,000 and $7,500 respectively, for
mounted , animal-drawn , and motorized units, and
providedfurther,the annual costof the use of ar-
mories , including rental and all costs of operation
and maintenance , insofar as such cost may be
charged against the Armory Fund , shallnot exceed
$2,000for each unit, and $1,500.00for each addi-
tionalunit at the same stationand using the same
facilities;provided,in thediscretionof theArmory
Commission,the annual costof armoriesand other
facilities for mounted and animal -drawn or motor-
ized units may be $2,500 and $2,000 for each addi-
tionalunit of the same classusing the same facili-
ties .
The amounts to be appropriatedout of the Ar

184 COMMONWEALTH OF KENTUCKY
mory Fund for the constructionand /or acquisition
of armories,and for the maintenance and /or rental
of armoriesshall,withinthe limitshereinbeforein
thissectionsetforth,be whollywithinthediscretion
of the Armory Commission .
§4 . There ishereby annuallyappropriated, out
of the generalexpenditurefund, a sum equal to
three (3 ) cents for each person who was a resident
of the State,as shown by each lastpreceding federal
census, to be known as the Armory Fund and to be
expended by the Armory Commission hereinbefore
in Section 1 created . Said fund shall be a continu-
ous fund and any balance unexpended at the end of
any fiscalyear shallremain in, and to the creditof,
said fund untilexpended .
§5 . The fiscalcourtof any county,and the gov-
erning body of any city or incorporatedtown,
wherein a unit or unitsof the Kentucky National
Guard have been or may hereafter be established,
may , either severally, or acting jointly with each
other or with the Armory Commission , constructor
acquireby purchase, contract, lease, gift, donation
or condemnation, grounds and/or buildingswhich
shallbe suitableforpublicassemblages,conventions,
exhibitionsand entertainments, provided that such
buildings, or the plans and specificationstherefor,
are firstapproved by the Armory Commission as
suitablefor use as armories by the National Guard
units establishedor to be establishedin such county,
cityor town,and provided further, that said fiscal
court and /or governing body of the city or town
shallhave contracted with the Armory Commission
for the use of such buildingsas an armory by such
NationalGuard unitor unitsupon terms not incon-
sistent with this Act .
For thepurpose of providingnecessaryfunds for
the acquisitionor constructionof suchbuildingsand

ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY 185
grounds ,and the cost of maintenance and operation
thcreof, such fiscalcourtand/or governingbody of
the cityor town is empowered to annually levy and
collecta taxnotexceedingten(10) centson each one
hundred dollars ($100) worth of taxable property
in such county and/or cityor town, as determined
by the last regular assessment for county or city
taxes; provided, however, if funds receivedfrom
donationsor from earningsfrom saidbuilding, to-
gether with rentalsreceivedfrom the Armory Com-
mission,are adequateto providefor allexpensesof
operationand maintenance, said tax leviesmay be
withheld and appropriatedto the generalexpense
fund of the county or city; and provided further,
that in event any fiscalcourt or other governing
body of a county,has, under pre-existinglaws, ac-
quired or constructedbuildingsand grounds suitable
for publicassemblages,conventions,exhibitionsand
entertainments,and alsofor armory purposes, and
shallcontract with the Armory Commission for the
use thereofby National Guard units upon terms not
inconsistentwith thisAct ,such fiscalcourt or other
governing body of the county may levy and collect
necessary taxes for the payment of expenses of oper-
ation and maintenance in an amount not to exceed
ten (10) centson each one hundred dollars($100)
worth of taxableproperty in said county.
$6. All property actuallyused for armory pur-
poses,as hereinabovedefined,shallbe exempt from
alltaxation,impost or assessment.
$ 7. The power of condemnationhereingranted
to the Armory Commission, and to counties, cities
and incorporated towns , shall be exercised in man-
ner prescribedin Section2852,Kentucky Statutes,
for the condemnation of property for park purposes
in cities of the first class .
$ 8. Whenever in the opinion of the Armory

186 COMMONWEALTH OF KENTUCKY
Commission itispracticableto do so, each armory
of the nationalguard shallcontaina room suitable
for meetings of associationscomposed of veterans
of the war between the states,the Spanish -American
war , and the world war , and shallbe availablefor
such meetings under such rules and regulations as
may be prescribedby the commanding officerof the
nationalguard unitor anitsusing such armory .
§9 . If any section,paragraph,clauseor separa-
bleprovisionof thisactshallbe heldto be invalid,
such fact shallnot affector render invalidany other
section,paragraph,clauseor separableprovisionof
the act,itbeing the intentionof the General Assem-
bly in enacting this act to enact each section, par-
agraph,clauseand provisionseparately.
$ 10 . This act,and alland each of itsvarious sec-
tionsand provisions, shallbe liberallyconstrued in
favor of the purposes of the act.
§ 11. All laws,or parts of laws, inconsistent, or
in conflictwith the provisions of thisact are hereby
repealed .
§ 12. Because the Court of Appeals of Kentucky
has declared unconstitutional section 2711a-128,
Kentucky Statutes, and there is no provisionsin
existinglaws for armories for the Kentucky Nation-
alGuard ,an emergency ishereby declaredto exist,
and this act shall take effecton and after its ap-
proval by the Governor .
Approved March 15 , 1924 .
CHAPTER 73 .
AN ACT to assist disabled men and women in obtaining from
the federal government the relief authorized by acts of Con-
gress , continuing in existence for that purpose the Kentucky
Disabled Men's Board , and appropriating funds therefor ; pro-
viding maintenance of an officeand stenographic help to carry
on work of said board , and declaring an emergency to exist.

ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY 187
Be itenacted by the General Assembly of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky :
§1 . That the Kentucky Disabled Ex -Service
Men's Board created by act approved February
27th, 1922,be continuedin existencefor an addi-
tionalperiodof two (2) years from the end of the
1924 session of the General Assembly of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky , for the purpose of assist-
ing disabledveteransof the World War , who are
residentsof Kentucky and who are or may be en-
titledto aid from the United States government pur-
suant to Federal legislationfor the benefitof dis-
abled ex-servicemen and women in preparing and
presentingtheirsaid claimsto the proper officers,
and in securing and expediting action thereon ; to
relieveand preventcongestionin the examination,
forwardingand actionon saidclaims; and to as far
as possible,cause every disabledveteranto receive
promptly and regularlysuch reliefas he isentitled
to .
§2 . Said Kentucky Disabled Ex -Service Men's
Board shallduring the time for which itsexistence
is extended by section1 of thisact,be composed of
three(3) members one of whom shallbe the Lieu-
tenantGovernor ofKentucky,anotherof whom shall
be the State Commander of the American Legion,
Department of Kentucky , and the third shall be
electedannuallyby thesetwo members . The mem-
bers of the board shallserve without salary or com-
pensation, but they shallbe paid their actual and
necessary travelingexpenses while in the discharge
of their officialduties .
3. The words “ ex-service men and women " as
used in thisact,includeany individualwho served
on activeduty in the Army , Navy or Marine Corps
of the United States,between the dates of April 6th,

188 COMMONWEALTH OF KENTUCKY
1917, and November 11th, 1918,inclusive, and who
has received an honorable discharge from such ser-
vice .
§4 . The board shall elect a secretary whose
dutiesshallbe toprepare inproper form,claimsof
disabledex-servicemen and women , to follow up
claimswhich have been delayed or neglected;to keep
in constant contact with the United States Veterans '

personallyvisitevery congressionaldistrictof the
Stateat leastonce every threemonths, and to per-
form such other duties as the board may direct. He
shallbe paid a salaryfixedby the board ,which shall
not exceed thirty-sixhundred dollars($3,600) per
annum ,and he shallbe paid his actualand necessary
travelingexpenses while engaged in the perform-
ance of his duties .
§5 . For thepurpose of carryingout the provis-
ionsof thisact,thereishereby appropriatedout of
Statefunds,not otherwiseappropriatedtotheKen-
tucky Disabled Ex -Service Men's Board the sum of
fifteenthousand dollars($15,000) per year for two
(2) years . The board shallkeep an accurate and de-
tailed account of allreceipts and disbursements,
and itsbooks shallbe subject to examination and
audit by the State Inspector and Examiner . Any
part of the fund unused at the expirationof the per-
iod for which the board is created shall be turned
back to the general State fund .
§6 . The Kentucky Disabled Ex -Service Men's
Board shallhave the power to expend such reason-
able sums as may be necessaryto employ clerical
and stenographic assistantsfor the secretaryof the
board, and to establishand maintain offices. Such
officesshall be maintained near or in conjunction

ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY 189
with the officesof State headquarters of the Ameri-
can Legion .
§7 . The Kentucky Disabled Ex -ServiceMen's
Board shallceaseto existon July 1st,1926.
§ 8. Alllaws and partsoflaws inconsistenthere-
with are hereby repealed.
§9 . There being many hundreds of disabled ex-
servicemen whose applicationsfor government aid
have not been actedupon due to congestion,lackof
knowledge of necessaryprocedure,and otherremed-
iable causes, and it being considered that if this
board ceasestofunction,thedisabledex-servicemen
will not receivethe information, assistanceand
prompt actionon theirclaims, which they should
have , an emergency is hereby declared to exist and
thisact shalltake effectfrom and after itspassage
and approval as required by law .
Approved March 21, 1924 .

CHAPTER 83 .
AN ACT to amend an Act entitled "An Act for the government
of cities of the Second Class ," approved March nineteenth ,
eighteen hundred and ninety -four, so as to enlarge the powers
of cities of the Second class and to provide for the elimination
of grade crossings of the public streets of such citiesat grade
over the tracks of steam or electric railroads , giving the gen-
eral Council or Board of City Commissioners power to order

ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY 219
and contract for the elimination of such crossings and power
to provide for the character of crossings to be substituted
therefor, and to designate the plans , specifications and terms
under which said work is to be done ; providing for mainten-
ance and reconstruction thereof ; and further to provide for
the method of apportioning the cost of such grade eliminations
as between such cities and the railroad companies and city
railway and interurban railway companies affected thereby ,
and providing further that such city shall be empowered to
issue bonds to provide for the cost of said city'sportion of any
such work , and to condemn land and property needed for such
work .
Be itenactedby theGeneralAssembly oftheCom
monwealth of Kentucky :
That an act entitled" An Act for the government
of citiesof the second class," approved March nine-
teenth, eighteen hundred and ninety-four, be
amended by adding thereafterthe following:
§1 . The generalcouncilor board of citycom-
missioners of any cityof the second classshallhave
power,when in itsjudgment itisreasonablyneces
sary for the safety of the public,to order the elimi-
nation of grade crossingsof public streetsover the
tracks of steam or electricrailroads, and for this
purposesaidgeneralcouncilor board of citycom-
missionersmay ,when authorizedby ordinance,con-
tractwith any steam railroadcompany or companies
and streetor interurbanrailwaycompanies thereby
affectedfor the eliminationof any crossing in any
cityof thesecondclass,where a publicstreetcrosses
at grade the tracksof any steam or electricrailroad,
on such plans, specificationsand terms as may be
agreed on between thecityand therailroadcompany
or companies and streetor interurban railway com-
panies affectedthereby. In event such a contract
is made the cost of elimination and construction
shallbe apportionedas provided in sectionsthree
(3) and four (4) hereof.

ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY 251
CHAPTER 92 .
AN ACT to prohibit the running of stock at large in towns of
the sixth class of this Commonwealth .
Be itenacted by the General Assembly of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky :
§1 . That the board of trustees of towns of the
sixthclassof thisCommonwealth are hereby empow-
ered to prohibithorses,cattle,hogs and otherstock
from running at large on the streetsand alleysof
such towns and may by ordinanceso provide. Said
board of trusteesmay by resolutionestablishand
maintaina pound,and make proper regulationsfor
impounding and keeping stockwhen found within
the limitsof said towns of the sixthclass,regardless
of whether or not the owners of said stock reside
within said said towns ;and for fixingfees of officers
for impounding, trialand releaseof same, as well
as penaltiesfor the violationof said ordinance.
§2 . All actsor parts of actsin conflictto thisact
are hereby repealed.
Approved March 28, 1924 .
CHAPTER 93 .
AN ACT providing for the creation of water districts, the ap-
pointment of water commissioners and specifying their powers
and duties .
Be itenactedby the General Assembly of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky :
§1 . Upon petition of twenty -five freeholders,
residentsof theproposed district,the countyjudge
may establisha water district.
§2 . The saidpetitionshalldescribetheterritory
intended to be included and shall set out the reasons
for the necessityof the organizationof such dis

252 COMMONWEALTH OF KENTUCKY
trict. Upon thefilingof saidpetition,the judge of
the county court shallcause to be publishedin a
newspaper of generalcirculationin the county, a
noticethat said petitionhas been filed. Within
thirtydays afterthepublication,any residentof the
proposed districtmay filehisobjection. The court
shallthen set the case for hearing within ten days .
If the court shallbe of the opinion that the establish-
ment of such districtisreasonably necessary for the
health, convenience and comfort of the residents
thereof,itshallthereupon make an order establish-
ing said district, and designatingthe same by a
name or number . The court may in this order
strikeoffany part of the territorywhich testimony
shows willnot be benefitedby the creation of such
district. If the court be not satisfiedthat said dis-
trictshallbe created, itshalldismissthe petition.
Either party may thereupon prosecute an appeal to
the circuit court for a retrial and from the circuit
court to the Court of Appeals in the manner now
providedby law.
§3 . After the countyjudge shallhave made an
order creatingsaidwater district,he shallappoint
threecommissioners,residentsof the district,to be
known as water districtcommissioners . One of said
commissionersshallbe appointedfor the term of
two years, one for the term of three years and one
for the term of four years. Upon the expiration of
the terms of officeof each of these commissioners the
county judge shallappoint a commissioner to suc-
ceed him to serve for the term of four years.
§4 . After the establishment of the water dis-
trict, its territoriallimits may be enlarged or di-
minished in the following way : The water commis-
sionersshallfilea petitionwith the county judge,
describing the territory sought to be annexed or
strickenoffand settingoutthereasonsforthesame.

ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY 253
Noticeof saidpetitionshallbe publishedin a news-
paper of generalcirculationinthecounty,and with-
in thirtydays afterthe publicationof such notice,
any residentof the water districtor other territory
proposed tobe annexed or strickenoff,may filehis
objections. The county judge shallthereupon set
the matter forhearing,and,ifhe be of the opinion
that the annexation of such territoryis reasonably
necessary,he shallmake an orderannexingsaiddis-
trict. Otherwisehe shalldismissthe petition. If

from the circuitcourt to the Court of Appeals in the
manner now provided by law .

mediately organizeby electinga chairman ,secretary
and treasurer. They shallhave power to acquire,
construct,maintainand operatea water system for
theirdistrictand to make contractswith municipal-
itiesand otherpersonsand corporations,for and in

one hundred ($100.00) dollarson propertyas asses-
sed for countypurposes and to issuebonds.It shall
be theduty annuallyof saidcommissioners,in mak-
ing out thetax roll,todeterminethe rateto be fixed
not exceeding fiftycents on one hundred dollarsas-
sessed for county purposes ,and to fixthe amount to
be paid by cach taxpayer within the district. After
such tax roll has been made out and on filein the of-
ficeof thecommissioners,itshallbe theduty of said

commissionersto advertisein some newspaper for
two issues,publishedwithinthe county, in general
terms, that the tax rollhas been made out by them
and on filein theiroffice,subjectto inspectionby all
taxpayers,and subjectto objectionor exceptionby
each taxpayeriftherebe any errorstherein. Within
fifteendays afterthe firstpublicationin saidnews-

inghisobjectionsor exceptionstotheamount of tax

ers shallafford him a fair and impartial trialand

determined the necessityfor the issuance and sale

CHAPTER 94 .

monwealth of Kentucky :

ercisedby a playground and recreationboard,such

pired term . Members shallserve without pay .

CHAPTER 95 .
AN ACT to amend and add to Chapter 89, Kentucky Statutes ,
title Municipal Corporations , and especially that part of said
chapter relating to cities of the third class, and to authorize
cities of the third class to acquire by purchase , or otherwise
and to operate street railroads and omnibus or taxicab lines
and their equipment and to issue and sell bonds to raise funds
for that purpose .
Whereas , it appears that in certain cities of the third class in
this Commonwealth there exists street car systems consisting of
tracks , cars, motive power and other appliances which have
ceased to be operated and which may not resume operation by
the present owners , and it being considered desirable by the
population of such city or citiesthat there should be maintained
therein street car systems or street omnibus or taxicab lines,
therefore--
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§1 . That any cityof the thirdclassin thisCom-
monwealth may by ordinanceof itscommon council
or board of commissioners,as thecasemay be,duly

ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY 259
passed and adopted,providethatsaidcitymay ac-
quire,and such citymay thereuponacquireby pur-
chase, lease or otherwise, any streetcar system ex-
istingin said city, with allitsappliances, or may
establishand installsuch system , and may operate
and improve and extend the same when so acquired
or when establishedand installedupon such terms
and conditionsas may be provided by ordinance and
by the terms of the contractby which such system
isacquiredor installed; and any such citymay ac-
quire,establish,installand operatea streetomnibus
or taxicab system for the accommodation of itsin-
habitantsand the travelingpublic,such omnibus or
taxicabsystem to operateupon such streets,at such
times, with such stationsand stopping places and
upon such terms for riding therein as the common
councilor board of commissioners, as the case may
be, shallprescribe by ordinance for that purpose .
§2 . In order to provide for the expense and
financingof such streetcar system or streetomni-
bus or taxicabline, such citymay issue and sell
bonds at not less than par and accrued interest, to
bear any rate of interest not exceeding five per
cent payable semi-annually, and to mature at any
time not exceeding twenty years after their date,
and may provide for a sinking fund to meet said
bonds at their maturity. But no such bonds shall
be issued except in compliance with the general law
of this Commonwealth in reference to the amount
of indebtednessthat may be incurred by citiesof the
thirdclass,nor untilaftersuch vote and electionas
may be required by law to authorize the incurring
of such indebtedness .
§3 . It appearing that there may exist an emer-
gency for the passage of thisact, an emergency is
declared and this act shall be in force from and after
itspassage . Approved March 15 , 1924 ,

ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY 277
CHAPTER 100 .
AN ACT relating to office and officers .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§1 . It shallbe unlawful for any justiceof the
peaceinany countyofthisCommonwealth tobecome
interested, eitherdirectlyor indirectly,in any con-
tractwhich may be letby the fiscalcourt with rela-
tion to the buildingof roads or any internalim-
provement,or to receive,directlyor indirectly,any
emoluments or benefitsfrom any contract so let
by the court with relationto the building of roads
or any public or internal improvement while such
justiceof the peace is acting as such officer.
§2 . It shallfurtherbe unlawfulfor any justice
of the peace in thisCommonwealth to work ,oversee,
supervise, or superintendany work on any public
road,bridge,culverts,fills,quarry pits or any other
road work or internalimprovement under any con-
tract,directlyor indirectlymade with the said fiscal
courtor for which the saidjusticeisto receiveany
compensation, directlyor indirectly, whilsthe is a
member of such fiscal court .
§3 . It shalllikewisebe unlawful for any jus-
ticeof the peace to furnish to the county any ma-
terialof any kind whatsoever to be used in any road
or bridge construction or other internal improve .
ment ,or forthebuildingof any work,thatis,roads,bridges or other internal improvement under
contract let by him , while member of the
fiscalcourt for hire: Provided, however, that
such material so used in the road constructionmight
be furnished as cheaply and as convenient for use
as might otherwisebe furnishedfor the purpose
desired .
a

278 COMMONWEALTH OF KENTUCKY
§4 . It shallbe unlawfulfor thecounty judge or
thecountyattorneyat any time whileactingas such
officerstobe directlyor indirectlyinterestedin any
contract so let by the fiscalcourt for any roads,
bridges, or parts thereof, or any other public or
internalimprovement,or to receiveany emoluments,
benefitsor pay,directlyor indirectly,by reason of
such work ,or to furnish any material or other thing
of value to be used in such work whilst either is
fillingthe officeaforesaid.
§5 . Any officerabove named who violatesany
of the provisionsof thisactshall,upon conviction,
be finednot less than $50.00nor more than $200.00,
or confinedin the county jailnot less than ten nor
more than fortydays, or both such finedand im-
prisoned in the discretionof the court or jury try-
ing the case,and,in addition,upon conviction,shall
forfeit his office.
Approved March 25, 1924 .

CHAPTER 103 .
AN ACT to define and standardize ice cream , prescribing when
it shall be deemed to be adulterated or misbranded , giving the
State Board of Health and its agents and local boards of health
all the right and powers to enforce the provisions of this act
and prevent violation of any of the provisions thereof that are
conferred on said State Board and said local boards and agents
by Section 2060 and Subsections 1 to 18 thereof , in the 1918
edition of the Kentucky Statutes , and fixing the same penalties
for adulterating or misbranding ice cream that are provided
for the adulteration or misbranding of food or drugs in said
Section 2060 .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§1 . That for the purpose of this act ice cream
is hereby defined and standardized:

284 COMMONWEALTH OF KENTUCKY
First. Ice cream is a frozen food product,varied
as to kind and proportionof ingredients,withinthe
limitsestablishedby custom and usage .
Second . Ice cream consistschieflyof a sweetened
and flavoredmixture of cream , or cream and milk,
or milk with or without added milk fat in the form
ofsound,sweetbutter;or as containedincondensed,
evaporated or concentrated milk or in milk powder ,
and with or without added milk solids not fat in the
form of skim milk powder or as contained in milk
powder or in condensed,evaporatedor concentrated
skim milk; or of a sweetened and flavored homo-
genized or emulsifiedmixture of sound sweet butter,
milk powder or skim milk powder and water,with
the additionof geletin, vegetablegum , or other
wholesome stabilizer .
Third . Standard ice cream shall contain not less
than ten (10) per cent milk fat and the contentof
milk fat and milk solids not fat combined shall be
not lessthan eighteen(18) per cent, except that
when the ingredientsof standard ice cream include
eggs, fruitor fruitjuice,cake, confection, cocoa or
chocolate,or nuts, such reduction of the percentage
of milk fat and milk solidsnot fat as may be due to
the addition of such ingredientsshallbe allowed.
Fourth . When ice cream is sold or offered for
salewithoutdesignationofitskind,qualityor grade
on a label, brand or tag attached to the package or
container, or, in case of removal from the original
package or container, by a notice conspicuously
posted in or at the place where such ice cream is
soldor offeredfor sale,itshallbe deemed thatsuch
ice cream is sold or offered for sale as standard ice
cream .
§2 . That for the purposes of thisact icecream
shall be deemed to be adulterated :
First. If in qualityor grade itislower than the

ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY 285
professedstandardof qualityor grade under which
it is sold or offered for sale .
Second . If it contains any added poisonous or
other added deleteriousingredientswhich may ren-
der such icecream injuriousto health.
Third. If itcontains any rancid or renovated or
processbutter,or any fator oilotherthan milk fat
and thefator oilor containedeggs and nutsand the
fat or oilof substances used for flavoringpurposes
only.
Fourth . If itconsistsin whole or in part of any
filthyor decomposed substancewhich may render
such icecream injuriousto health,or is otherwise
so contaminatedthatsuch icecream isinjuriousto
health .
§3 . That for the purpose of thisact icecream
shall be deemed to be misbranded :
First. If the label, brand, tag or noticeunder
which it is sold or offered for sale is false or mis-
leadingin any particularas to the kind, grade or
quality,or compositionof such icecream ; or ifany
notice to the purchaser required by this act to be
given isomitted.
Second . If it is sold or offered for sale as the
product of one manufacturer when in realityitis
the product of another manufacturer ; or if on the
label,brand,tag or noticeunder which itissoldor
offeredforsalethereisany falsestatementconcern-
ing the sanitaryconditionsunder which such ice
cream is manufactured .
§4 . It shallbe unlawful for any person,persons,
firm or corporationwithin thisState to manufacture
for sale,produce for sale,expose for sale,have in
his or theirpossessionfor sale, or to sellany ice
cream that is adulterated or misbranded within the
meaning of thisact,and any person,persons,firm or
corporationviolatingthe provisionsof thisact or

286 COMMONWEALTH OF KENTUCKY
any of them shall be fined not less than ten dollars
nor more than one hundred dollars,or be imprisoned
not to exceed fiftydays , or both so fined and im-
prisoned, for each offense; provided that no ice
cream shall be deemed misbranded or adulterated
within the provisions of thisact when itisintended
for shipment to or salein any other stateor country,
when the ice cream is not so adulterated or mis-
branded as to be in conflictwith the laws of the
United States,but ificecream,althoughitmay be
manufactured or produced with the intention of
shipment to or salein some otherstateor country,
be, in fact, sold or offered for sale in this State,
then thisprovisoshallnot affordany immunity.
$ 5. The State Board of Health and its agents,
and localboards of health are given allthe rights
and powers to enforcetheprovisionsof thisactand
to prevent any violationof any of the provisions
thereof that are conferred on the State Board
of Health and its agents and local boards of
health by section2060 and subsections1 to 18 there-
of,inthe1918 edtionof theKentucky Statutesin re-
spect to food and drugs .
This actshalltakeeffectaccordingto the provis-
ions of the Constitution .
Approved March 25 , 1924 .

CHAPTER 107 .
AN ACT authorizing the sale of property heretofore or hereafter
acquired and owned by any county or counties of this state,
to be used for or to establish a sanatorium for the treatment
of tuberculosis, where such property has ceased to be used for
such purpose , or in the judgment of the fiscal court of any
such county or counties the property so acquired and owned
by it or them is not being and cannot be maintained and suc-
cessfully used for such purpose .
Be itenacted by the General Assembly of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky :
§1 . That whenever any county or counties in
thisState have heretofore or may hereafter acquire
and own any property for the purpose of forming a
district for the erection and maintenance of a tuber-
culosissanatorium ,and such property has ceased to

312 COMMONWEALTH OF KENTUCKY
be used for such purpose,itmay ,upon the petition
of the fiscalcourt of such county or counties, be
ordered to be soldby the circuitcourt of such county,
or,where the districtis composed of more than one
county the circuitcourt of either of said counties,
and the net proceeds paid into the proper county
treasury or treasuries .
The board of trustees of such district shall be
made defendants to the petitionor if the trustees
have resignedor ceasedto act,then the proceeding
may be by an ex partepetitionby thefiscalcourt,in
either case the court to be satisfied that the facts
statedin thepetitionare substantiallytrue and com-
ply with thisact. And the court being so satisfied
shall decree the sale of such property upon such
terms and conditionsas may be justand proper and
shallmake such orders as may be necessary to in-
vestthepurchaserwith the titleto saidproperty.
§2 . If any such districtis composed of more
than one county the net proceeds of such sale, after
paying allcourtcosts,justdebts and claims, shall
be dividedbetween thecountiesin theproportionin
which theycontributedtowards thepurchaseofsuch
property and the constructionof said sanatorium .
§3 . In case such sale is decreed and made as
herein provided, then the sanatorium districtin
which such propertyislocatedshallbe therebydis-
continued .
§4 . All laws and parts of laws of the Common-
wealth in conflictwith this act are hereby repealed.
Approved March 15 , 1924 .
CHAPTER 108 .
AN ACT to amend and re-enact sections forty-one hundred and
forty-three and forty-one hundred and forty-eight of the Ken-
tucky Statutes, John D. Carroll'sedition, nineteen hundred

ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY 313
and fifteen, relating to revenue and taxation, and changing the
time for the collection of taxes .
Be itenacted by the General Assembly of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky :
§1 . That section forty-one hundred and forty-
three of the Kentucky Statutes, John D. Carroll's
edition, nineteen hundred and fifteen, be and the
same ishereby amended so as to read as follows,to-
wit :
" Section forty-one hundred and forty-two . (Fif-
teen) Payment of Taxes and Reports by Sheriff-
Penalty. The sheriffor collectorof State revenues
in each county of thisCommonwealth shall, on the
firstday ofMay ,June,August,September,October,
November , December and January , and February ,
under oath,reporttotheAuditor ofPublicAccounts
the amount of alltaxes he has collectedand pay the
same immediately, and shallaccount for and pay
all taxes which he has collected for the State into
the State Treasury by December first in each
year;and upon hisfailureto do so he and hissure-
tiesshallbe liabletherefor, and shallbe proceeded
against at the firstterm thereafterof the Franklin
circuitcourt. Any sheriffor collectorwho shall
failto report as herein required shallbe liableto
indictmentin the Franklin circuitcourt, and fined
not less than one hundred dollars nor more than five
hundred dollarsfor each offense,and itshallbe the
duty of the Auditor of Public Accounts to report to
the grand jury of Franklincounty at the next term
of said court aftersuch failureto report. The sher-
iffor collectorshallbe required by the Auditor of
PublicAccounts to pay a penaltyof sixper centum
on all taxes collectedand unpaid by him on the
thirty-firstday ofJanuary ineachyear. The Audi-
tor of Public Accounts with his settlement with the

314 COMMONWEALTH OF KENTUCKY
sheriffor collector,shallchargehim with thepenal-
tiesaccruing under the provisions of this act."
§2 . That sectionforty-one hundred and forty-
eight of the Kentucky Statutes, John D. Carroll's
edition, nineteen hundred and fifteen, be and the
same ishereby amended so as to read as follows,to-
wit :
" Section forty-one hundred and forty-eight.
(Twenty ) Taxes when Due - Interestand Penalty-
All State,countyand districttaxes,exceptas other-
wise speciallyprovided shallbe due and payable on
and after the firstday of March after the assess-
ment ,and alltaxes which are not paid on the thirty-
firstday of December after the same are due shall
be deemed delinquent, and such taxes shallbear in-
terestat the rate of sixper cent per annum from the
thirty-firstday ofDecember afterthey are due until
paid; and any person or personsfailingtopay their
taxesby thethirty-firstday of December intheyear
followingthe assessmentfor such taxes, shallpay
a penaltyof sixper centum additionalon the taxes
due and unpaid . The sheriffor collectorwhose duty
it is to receive or collectthe taxes shall collect the
interestand penalty and account for the same in the
same way which they are requiredto collectand ac-
count for the taxes ."
§2a . That the provisionsof this act shallbe
effectiveand in fullforce,untilJanuary one,nine-
teen hundred and twenty -six.
§3 . All laws or partsoflaws inconflictwith the
provisionsofthisactarehereby repealed.
Approved March 22, 1924 .

Attached to the foregoing Act is the following veto message
signed by the Governor of Kentucky , March 24, 1924 :
House Bill No. 534 having been delivered in my office on
March 13 , 1924 , and having been examined by me , I disapprove
of Section 37 , sub -section D thereof , appropriating to the Uni-
versity of Kentucky the sum of Fifteen Thousand ($15,000.00)
Dollars for the purpose of making investigations looking to the
prevention of diseases of tobacco , and other matters pertaining
thereto, because , in my judgment , the Experimental Station of
the University of Kentucky is and has been amply provided for,
and has sufficientfunds to make any investigations it deems ad-
visable to make in the interest of Agriculture .
In view of the present financial condition of the State on the
one hand , and the fact that the need is not imperative on the
other ,I feel impelled to disapprove sub -section F of Section 37 of
House Bill 534 that seeks to appropriate Forty -five Thousand
($45,000.00) Dollars for the construction of a Chemistry building
for the University of Kentucky .
With reference to Sub -section A of section 38 of House Bill
534. I approve of the Sixty Thousand ($60,000.00) Dollars recom

374 COMMONWEALTH OF KENTUCKY
mended by the Budget Commission for the benefit of the State
Board of Health , and disapprove of the additional Ten Thousand
($10,000.00) Dollars added to the amount so appropriated by the
Budget Commission by the State Legislature by an amendment .
I further disapprove of sub -section F of Section 38 of House
Bill 534 which appropriated to the State Board of Health to be
used as a payment on the building located at Sixth and Main
Streets, Louisville, Kentucky , in the sum of Four Thousand
Seven Hundred and sixty ($4,760.58) Dollars and fifty-eight cents ,
for the reason that a recent legislative investigation committee
recommended a legal investigation of the title to said prop-
erty by the Attorney General of Kentucky , and his investigation
of said title has not as yet been made .
I also disapprove of Sub - section B of Section 42 of House Bill
534 , which appropriates Seventeen Thousand Five Hundred
($17,500.00 ) Dollars to the Department of the State Geologist
for cooperative work with the United States Geological Survey ,
other provision having been made for this Department by a
separate Act of the recent Legislature .
I disapprove of all of the foregoing sub -sections of House Bill
No. 534 , in addition to the reasons hereinbefore set out , because
the financial condition of the State does not , at this time , permit
these expenditures , and I therefore veto the sub -sections of the
sections heretofore mentioned .
All of the other provisions and appropriations contained in
House Bill No. 534 are hereby approved .
W. J. FIELDS , Governor of Kentucky .
March 24 , 1924 .
